explanation: <Commence> is the formal equivalent of 'begin' or 'start', used in official contexts.
<Start> is the everyday, informal way to say 'begin'.
<Terminate> is formal for 'end' or 'stop', common in contracts and legal documents.
<Finish up> is a casual phrasal verb meaning to complete something.
<Endeavour> is a formal word meaning to try hard, used in academic and professional writing.
<Give it a go> is a casual expression meaning to try something.
<Ascertain> means to find out or determine with certainty, used in formal investigations.
<Figure out> is the informal equivalent of 'ascertain' or 'determine'.
<Subsequently> is a formal adverb meaning 'after that', used in academic writing.
<After that> is the simple, everyday way to indicate sequence.
<Notwithstanding> means 'despite' or 'in spite of', common in legal and academic texts.
<Even so> is a casual way to express concession, similar to 'nevertheless'.
<Remuneration> is the formal term for payment or salary, used in HR and contracts.
<Pay> is the common, everyday word for money received for work.
<Pursuant to> means 'in accordance with', used in legal and regulatory documents.
<According to> works in both formal and informal contexts to cite a source.
<Facilitate> means to make a process easier, common in business and academic contexts.
<Help out> is the casual phrasal verb equivalent of 'assist' or 'facilitate'.
<Aforementioned> refers to something stated earlier, used in legal and academic writing.
This casual phrase is how we refer back to something in everyday conversation.
<Regarding> means 'about' or 'concerning' and works across formal and informal registers.
<With respect to> is a formal prepositional phrase meaning 'regarding' or 'concerning'.
<About> is the simplest, most informal way to introduce a topic.
<In lieu of> means 'instead of', commonly found in formal documents and contracts.
<Instead of> is a neutral expression that works in any context.
<Henceforth> means 'from this point on', used in proclamations and legal texts.
<From now on> is the everyday way to say 'starting from this moment'.
<Nevertheless> is a formal connector meaning 'despite that', used in essays and reports.
<Still> can express concession in both formal and informal contexts.
<But> is the most basic and informal way to express contrast.
<Enquire> is the formal British English spelling of 'inquire', used in official correspondence.
<Ask> is the simplest and most informal way to request information.
<Furthermore> adds information in formal writing, stronger than 'also' or 'and'.
<Also> is a neutral adverb for adding information, suitable for any register.
<Plus> is an informal connector used in casual speech to add information.
<Prior to> means 'before', used in formal and academic writing.
<Before> is a neutral preposition of time that works in any context.
<Obtain> is the formal equivalent of 'get', used in official and academic contexts.
<Get> is one of the most versatile and informal verbs in English.
<In the event that> is a formal conditional phrase meaning 'if', used in contracts.
